gaps.description: The Bill of Rights (version 1.0.0-seed) is an empty scaffold. All six articles — Scope of Enterprise, Prohibited Activities, Core Identity, Human Authority Reservation, Amendment Protocol, and Interpretation Rules — are marked '[To be defined by human principal]'. Without a defined BoR, Arbiter (#190) has no enterprise perimeter to enforce at scope queries. The MSP is explicitly 'strictly subordinate to the Bill of Rights' and any MSP rule that conflicts with the BoR 'is void on its face.' With the BoR empty, the entire constitutional foundation of the DIO is absent. The MSP cannot meaningfully constrain anything because there is no higher constitutional authority to be subordinate to. The system is operating without a defined enterprise scope, without prohibited activities, and without a core identity.

Article V of the Bill of Rights, which defines the Amendment Protocol for the BoR itself, is '[To be defined by human principal].' This creates a structural deadlock: the BoR needs to be filled with substantive constitutional content, but the process by which the human principal amends the BoR does not exist yet. The BoR's own authority header states 'Human principal only. No automated system may amend this document.' However, without Article V defining how amendments are proposed, reviewed, approved, hash-registered, and activated, there is no actionable path to move the BoR from scaffold to active constitution. The MSP amendment protocol is well-defined (PEM → Legislator → review → Rollout), but that pipeline explicitly cannot be used to expand enterprise scope — only BoR amendment can do that, and BoR amendment has no defined pipeline.

The MSP contains zero operational rules. The document explicitly states: 'The bootstrap seed contains no rules. The DIO operates under default-permissive MSP evaluation until Senate publishes substantive rules via the standard PEM → Legislator → review → Rollout flow.' Nomos (#200) evaluates MSP compliance at every Action Proposal adjudication as the second of two compliance gates. With no rules, every AP passes the MSP gate automatically. There are no operational constraints, no safety rules, no risk-tier constraints — nothing to prevent or even evaluate any action the DIO might propose. The genome references section is also empty, meaning no organ-specific operating rules exist either.

The MSP states that Arbiter (#190) 'verifies the Bill of Rights at every scope query' and that the BoR perimeter is the first compliance gate for every AP. However, the BoR has no defined Scope of Enterprise (Article I) and no Prohibited Activities (Article II). Arbiter is verifying a document with no boundaries. Every scope query effectively returns an unbounded permission set. Combined with the empty MSP rules (GAP-003), this means both governance gates — scope and compliance — pass automatically. The DIO is operating without any constitutional or operational constraints on its actions.

Spine state transitions show all governance organs — Arbiter (#190), Nomos (#200), Senate (#220), and Cerberus — are rapidly oscillating between ALIVE and DEGRADED states every ~30 seconds with 'missed_pong' / 'pong_recovered' transitions. This pattern is continuous and affects every organ in the system. If any governance organ is in DEGRADED state at the moment Nomos needs to adjudicate an AP, the authorization pipeline could be compromised. The MSP requires Nomos to evaluate every AP against MSP rules and Arbiter to verify every scope query — both functions depend on their respective organs being ALIVE at adjudication time.

Radiant stats report: scheduled='disabled', last_dream=null, health='never_run'. The dream cycle is the DIO's memory consolidation mechanism — it triages context blocks, promotes relevant ones to memory, prunes expired entries, and consolidates related memories. Without it running, context accumulates indefinitely (no pruning), memory degrades (no consolidation), and the DIO's working knowledge becomes stale or bloated. The MSP doesn't directly mandate dream cycles, but Radiant's memory/context management is foundational to the DIO's ability to maintain coherent state and make informed governance decisions.

Recent context shows a pattern of SafeVault backup failures spanning multiple days (2026-04-27 through 2026-05-03). Issues include: NAS mount failures (recurring — Operation not permitted), zombie backup processes blocking subsequent runs (two processes from 2026-05-02 alive for ~25 hours), script bugs (unbound variable ORGAN_SQLITE_DBS), and git sync non-fast-forward divergences on AOS-coretex-rtime. The most recent backup on 2026-05-03 was 'hung_then_killed' with only Layer 1 partially completed and Layers 2-3 never started. While SafeVault is not directly governed by the MSP, persistent backup failures threaten the DIO's ability to recover from failures — a prerequisite for the governance system to operate with integrity.

The MSP defines a detailed amendment pipeline: Policy Exception Messages (PEMs) are issued by Nomos when an AP is blocked → Senate-Legislator drafts a minimal diff → draft enters review state → HOM notification emitted to human-principal mailbox → activation requires POST /amend/review with decision=approve → Senate-Rollout executes staging → canary → activation → rollback pipeline → hash-verified via Graph adapter → recorded as append-only msp_version. However, zero PEMs have been issued (because zero MSP rules exist to block any AP — see GAP-003). The entire governance amendment pipeline — from PEM issuance through Rollout activation — has never been exercised. When the first substantive MSP rule is eventually created, the pipeline's first real execution will be its first test.

The BoR is entirely a scaffold with no articles filled (Articles I–VI undefined). Consequently, the MSP cannot derive any constitutional constraints for operational priority, prohibited activities during degraded states, or core identity safeguards. This absence leaves operational decision-making without principled boundaries, especially visible in the unconstrained flapping of governance organs (Senate, Arbiter, Nomos) that undermines the system's legislative and compliance integrity.",

      "urgency": 4,
      "impact": 4,
      "priority_score": 80
